steps.title: Tools that work WITH your functions

narration.default: You've seen sum, max, min, len, sorted — those work on data alone. Today's tools work WITH a function you wrote yourself: map applies your function to every item in a list, filter keeps only the items your function approves of. Plus zip and enumerate, which pair lists up neatly. Together they often replace a whole for loop with one short line.

Type:

num = -5
print("Absolute value:", abs(num))

abs is standard Python — it removes the sign. abs(-5) gives 5; abs(5) gives 5; abs(0) gives 0. Tiny tool, useful when you don't care about direction, only distance.

steps.title: map — apply a function to every item

narration.default:
Type:

numbers = [1, 2, 3]

def double(x):
    return x * 2

print("Doubled:", list(map(double, numbers)))

map takes two arguments — a FUNCTION and a LIST. It runs the function on every item and gives back a new sequence of results. We wrap it in list(...) to force the results out into a real list. Result: [2, 4, 6] — every number doubled, no for loop in sight.

steps.title: filter — keep only what passes the test

narration.default:
Type:

def greater_than_one(x):
    return x > 1

print("Filtered:", list(filter(greater_than_one, numbers)))

filter also takes a function and a list, but the function must return True or False. Only items where the function returns True survive. greater_than_one is True for 2 and 3, False for 1 — so the result is [2, 3].

Type:

names = ["Aman", "Riya", "Kabir"]
scores = [90, 85, 88]

print("Paired:", list(zip(names, scores)))

zip pairs items from two lists by position — first with first, second with second. Result: [('Aman', 90), ('Riya', 85), ('Kabir', 88)] — a list of tuples. Useful for printing a table, or for matching things up after sorting them separately.

Type:

print("With index:", list(enumerate(names)))

enumerate pairs every item with its position number — starting at 0. Result: [(0, 'Aman'), (1, 'Riya'), (2, 'Kabir')]. Useful when you want to loop over a list and ALSO know how far along you are: "3rd in line, here is your name".

steps.title: Run and watch one-liners replace loops

narration.default: Click Run. The terminal shows the absolute value, the doubled list, the filtered list, the paired tuples, and the indexed pairs. Each result that would have taken 3-5 lines of for loop fits in one line. That's why these tools are favourites of experienced Python programmers.

Quick guide. map: transform every item the same way. filter: drop items that fail a test. zip: pair lists up by position. enumerate: tag each item with its index. abs: drop the sign. As your programs grow these will save you typing — and once you spot the pattern, your code reads more like a sentence than a recipe.
